items.q: If one EM wave has a higher frequency than another, what is true about its wavelength?
items.correct: It is shorter — wavelength and frequency are inversely related (c = f x lambda).
items.misconception: It is also longer, because both increase together.
items.random_wrong: It is unchanged — only the speed differs.
items.why: Students treat all wave properties as moving together (more of one = more of the other), missing the fixed-speed constraint that ties them inversely.

items.q: When you push a wall, what does Newton's third law say about the wall?
items.correct: The wall exerts an equal and opposite force on you at the same instant.
items.misconception: The wall only pushes back if your force is strong enough to make it react.
items.random_wrong: The wall pushes back with a force proportional to how stiff it is, not equal to your force.
items.why: Students think action-reaction is causal and sequential — push first, then the wall 'responds' — instead of being two aspects of one interaction.

items.q: If a car doubles its speed, what happens to its kinetic energy?
items.correct: It quadruples (because KE = 1/2 mv^2 — kinetic energy is proportional to v^2).
items.misconception: Kinetic energy doubles because energy is proportional to speed.
items.random_wrong: Kinetic energy stays the same because mass hasn't changed.
items.why: Students assume linear relationships (double the input, double the output) and forget the v^2 factor in the kinetic energy formula.
It's v = H0 d. “v = m c^2” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Light year is a unit of:
items.why: Correct answer: distance. “time” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Orbit r=2x10^7 m, T=1x10^5 s. v (m/s)?
items.why: Exact answer: 1257. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
